mnemonics.title: POPE
mnemonics.content: POPE stands for the two main types of projections: Parallel and Orthographic, Perspective and Oblique - helping you remember all four major projection types in computer graphics.

mnemonics.title: MVP Matrix
mnemonics.content: Model-View-Projection: Remember the transformation pipeline order - first Model (object to world), then View (world to camera), finally Projection (camera to screen). Think 'MVP transforms objects to your screen!'

mnemonics.title: COPS
mnemonics.content: Center Of Projection - COPS helps remember that perspective projections have a finite center point (like a camera lens), while parallel projections have the center at infinity.

mnemonics.title: Vanishing Point Principle
mnemonics.content: Remember: In perspective projection, parallel lines converge to vanishing points. Think 'Vanquished Parallel Lines Meet at Vanishing Points' - VPLMVP.

mnemonics.title: NFF
mnemonics.content: Near Far Front - Remember the viewing frustum parameters: NEAR (closest plane), FAR (farthest plane), FRONT (front clipping plane). Keep them in mind to avoid clipping issues!

acronyms.usage: Use when mapping 2D world coordinates to screen coordinates. The window defines what portion of the scene to display, the viewport defines where on the screen to display it.
A normalized cube (typically -1 to 1 in x, y, z) used in the viewing pipeline after projection transformation. Makes clipping and further transformations easier.
The angular extent of the scene visible through the camera. Larger FOV = wider angle lens = more distortion at edges. Used in perspective projection calculations.
The coordinate system after perspective division. X and Y range from -1 to 1, Z from 0 to 1. This is the intermediate step before window-to-viewport transformation.
The point from which projection lines emanate (perspective) or the direction from which they come (parallel). Determines the type of projection and visual effect.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine looking through a window. Objects farther away appear smaller, and parallel lines (like railroad tracks) seem to converge at a point on the horizon - that's your vanishing point. The center of the window is your projection plane, your eye is the center of projection.

Think of technical engineering drawings. Lines that are parallel in 3D remain parallel in the 2D projection (no vanishing points). Orthographic shows true dimensions; oblique shows depth at an angle (like isometric drawings).

Picture a pyramid with its tip cut off (truncated pyramid). The tip represents the camera/eye position, the larger base represents the far clipping plane. The frustum defines what gets rendered - anything outside is clipped.

Imagine a photographer's viewfinder (window) being projected onto different sizes of photo paper (viewport). The relative positions stay the same, but the scale changes. Think of it as a digital zoom without changing the scene.
Think of the viewing frustum as a glass box. Any part of your 3D object outside this box gets 'cut off' before being flattened to 2D. Only what's inside the box reaches the screen.

analogies.analogy: Just like a physical camera captures a 3D world onto 2D film, the viewing transformation in CG acts as a virtual camera. The camera position is your eye, the film is your screen, and the lens determines the field of view (FOV).

Imagine holding a 3D object under a lamp. Its shadow on the wall is a 2D projection! Perspective is like a point light source (shadow distorts), parallel is like sunlight (rays are nearly parallel, minimal distortion).

The viewport is like your canvas or screen - it's where the final 'painting' appears. The window determines WHAT part of the world to paint, the viewport determines HOW BIG to paint it on your canvas.

Moving the world in front of a stationary camera gives the same result as moving the camera through the world. This is the key insight - world-to-camera transformation is just an inverse camera movement.
Think of depth sorting like stacking playing cards. The Z-buffer is like remembering exactly how far away each card is, so you know which one to draw on top when they overlap.
